RESULTSSpatial and molecular profiling reveals clinically relevant tumor 
ecosystem niches in treatment -naive high -grade serous carcinoma
Ziqi Kang1, Andreas Hainari1, Venla Kaislo1, Lina Maltrovsky1, Saundarya Shah1, Anastasia Lundgren1, Essi Kahelin 1,4,5, 
Matilda Salko1, Anni Virtanen3, Ulla -Majia Haltia3, Anniina Färkkilä1,2,3
1 Research Program in Systems Oncology, University of Helsinki, Finland 2 Institute for Molecular Medicine Finland, Helsinki Institute of Life Sciences, University of Helsinki, Finland 
3 Department of Obstetrics and Gynecology, Helsinki University Hospital, Finland 4 Department of Pathology, University of Hel sinki 
5 HUS Diagnostic Center, Helsinki University Hospital. The authors declare no conficts of interests.
METHODS INTRODUCTION
High-grade serous carcinoma (HGSC) shows high variability in therapeutic 
response and survival. Genomic instability may underlie part of this 
heterogeneity by driving tumor cell diversity and thus shaping the tumor 
microenvironment (TME) . However, how tumor molecular features relate to 
spatial TME organization remains unclear. 
In this project, We aim to quantify physical and functional TME states in 
treatment -naive HGSC and determine how tumor molecular features shape 
the ecosystem. Fig 1. Cohort of HGSC

Fig 4. Recurrent cellular neighbourhood (RCN) identified a stable tumor -stroma interface (TSI) niche, and links sample -
level reduced immune -cell proximity to the TSI with disease progression, especially in HRP samples. 
ACKNOWLEDGEMENTFig 5. Immune community captures higher -order spatial TME 
struture with different lymphoid -myeloid interaction patterns 
across HRD/HRP subgroups. 
CONCLUSION
•Spatial proteomics resolves HRD/HRP -associated immune TME states in HGSC. 
•Progression is associated with altered spatial immune architecture, marked by reduced immune -cell proximity to the tumor –
stroma interface and distinct lymphoid –myeloid community structures, especially in HRP tumors .
•Future work will integrate FGA and Xenium -defined niche programs to identify mechanisms linking genomic instability to 
spatial immune escape.
